results.possibleIdentity: Robert Fiala (also referred to as Bob Fiala) was a multimedia artist, director, and filmmaker active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a collaborator in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and was involved in the Intermedia '68 project at the Brooklyn Academy of Music, where he collaborated on kinetic dance and film projects. Fiala specialized in innovative cinematic techniques, including cinemagraphics, experimental macrophotography using polarized light, and animation. In 1969–1970, he was awarded a bursary from the Canada Council for the Arts in the category of "Art and Technology" while based in Montreal. By the early 1970s, Fiala became a key member of the New York-based Multigravitational Group (also known as Aerodance), serving as its Assistant Artistic Director and Company Manager. The group was noted for its "aerodance" performances, which utilized scaffolding and suspended structures to explore the intersection of dance, sculpture, and technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Larry Silverstein was a scientist and engineer associated with the Polytechnic Institute of Brooklyn (now NYU Tandon School of Engineering) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is known for his research in physics and electronics, most notably collaborating with Gordon Gould (the inventor of the laser) and Donald Scarl on experiments involving the Schwarz-Hora effect, which examined the modulation of electron beams by laser light. This work, conducted around 1969-1971, placed him at the intersection of advanced technology and experimental research. Given his background at Brooklyn Polytechnic—an institution that provided many technical collaborators for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) projects—and the era's focus on laser and electronic art, he is the most likely individual to have participated as an engineer-collaborator in E.A.T. initiatives, which sought to pair technical experts with artists like Robert Rauschenberg and Robert Whitman for performance and technical art pieces.

Lawrence (Larry) Richard Silverstein (1941–2025) was an American art educator and artist based in Maplewood, New Jersey. He graduated from the Newark School of Fine Art and Jersey City State College in 1966 with a degree in arts education. Silverstein had a long career as an art teacher in Franklin Township, NJ, spanning 38 years. Parallel to his teaching, he was active in the NJ art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s, exhibiting his work at various art shows and joining the First Mountain Crafters artist guild. He also worked as a movie theater projectionist at the Maplewood Movie Theater from 1968 to 1988, a role that involved technical mastery of film projection technology during the era of experimental multi-media and expanded cinema.

results.possibleIdentity: Jane Holzer, often referred to by the nickname "Baby Jane" Holzer, is an American art collector, film producer, and former model and actress who became a central figure in the 1960s New York avant-garde scene. Born Jane Brukenfeld in 1940, she was famously dubbed Andy Warhol’s first "superstar" and the "Girl of the Year" in 1964 by writer Tom Wolfe. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was a prominent member of Warhol’s Factory, appearing in his early experimental films such as "Soap Opera" (1964), "Couch" (1964), "Batman Dracula" (1964), and "Camp" (1965). Her association with Warhol placed her at the intersection of various experimental movements, including collaborations involving technology like Warhol's "Silver Clouds" (1966), which was created in partnership with Billy Klüver, a founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Holzer transitioned from a muse and performer to a significant art collector and consultant, acquiring works by Warhol, Keith Haring, and Jean-Michel Basquiat. In the 1970s and 80s, she moved into film production, with credits including the award-winning "Kiss of the Spider Woman" (1985). She remains an influential figure in the art world, serving as a trustee for the Norton Museum of Art.

results.possibleIdentity: Chris Hill (also known as Chris Hill) is a prominent media curator, artist, and educator who has specialized in the history of early video art and the intersection of art and technology from the late 1960s and 1970s. She is widely recognized for her work curating and documenting the first decade of video art (1968–1980), notably through the 17-hour anthology "Surveying the First Decade: Video Art and Alternative Media in the U.S." (1996/2008). Her research and writing, such as the essay "Attention! Production! Audience!—Performing Video in its First Decade, 1968-1980," extensively cover the legacy of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the cultural shifts of that era. Hill served as the Video Curator at Hallwalls Contemporary Arts Center in Buffalo, New York, for over a decade and has held faculty positions at Antioch College and CalArts. She was a student and colleague of early media pioneers Steina and Woody Vasulka and has been instrumental in the preservation and dissemination of their technological experiments and archives.

Charles Christopher Hill is an artist and photographer who was active in the early 1970s art scene, particularly in Los Angeles. He is a contemporary and close associate of performance artist Chris Burden, and he famously photographed many of Burden’s seminal works and events during this period (such as "Shoot," 1971). Hill’s own artistic practice is rooted in process-oriented art, including painting and printmaking, and he has been associated with the development of the contemporary art scene in Southern California from the early 1970s onward. His work is held in several museum collections, including the Hammer Museum and the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA).

results.possibleIdentity: Ron Cooper (born 1943) is an American visual artist primarily associated with the 'Light and Space' and 'Finish Fetish' movements that emerged in Southern California during the late 1960s and early 1970s. His early career featured work that explored perceptual phenomena using materials such as fluorescent light, neon, glass, and resin. Cooper was a participant in the landmark 'Art and Technology' program (1967–1971) at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA), an initiative that fostered collaborations between artists and industrial engineers, paralleling the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His Los Angeles studio was a hub for experimental performance, hosting dance workshops for figures like Alex Hay, who was a core participant in the foundational E.A.T. event '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering.' Additionally, an artist identified as 'R. Cooper' was featured in the May 1967 issue of Esquire in an article titled 'Andy Warhol’s Dirty Half Dozen,' which highlighted avant-garde performers and artists—including Carolee Schneemann and Charlotte Moorman—who were closely associated with E.A.T. and the Judson Dance Theater circle. Cooper's work has been included in retrospective exhibitions regarding the history of E.A.T., such as 'The Story of E.A.T.' at the Spencer Museum of Art. In 1990, he founded the well-known mezcal brand Del Maguey.

results.possibleIdentity: Barry Bryant (1940–1997) was an American artist, filmmaker, and musician active in New York City during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known as a mentor and manager for the pioneering electronic band Silver Apples, whom he hosted in his studio in 1967 and 1968, encouraging them to develop their experimental sound using oscillators and drums. Bryant famously spray-painted his entire penthouse silver for the band's debut party in 1968 and acted as a producer on their early albums. His own artistic practice included painting and filmmaking, and he was part of the experimental New York art scene associated with Fluxus and the cross-pollination of art and technology. In 1973, he founded the Samaya Foundation, a non-profit organization dedicated to making Tibetan Buddhist art and culture accessible, particularly through the creation of sand mandalas. He collaborated with avant-garde composer Pauline Oliveros on projects involving Tibetan ritual music and authored the scholarly book 'The Wheel of Time Sand Mandala'. His work and papers are included in historical archives such as the Gilbert and Lila Silverman Fluxus Collection at MoMA and the Fales Library at NYU.
